narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your Year 1 grades, which placed you in the 2nd decile at your school—well below the typical 6th decile average—and your Year 3 grades, which landed in the 1st decile compared to the usual 6th decile. It also considered your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, lower than the average 3.21, and your LSAT score of 27, significantly under the dataset's average of about 37. Overall, the model predicted an 82.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize admits likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your low Year 3 decile ranking, as it strongly signaled potential struggles in advanced coursework compared to peers who typically rank much higher. Your Year 1 decile also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance from early on. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the third-strongest influence but worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since it showed some resilience despite being below average. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school rankings stand out as weaker than the solid mid-range performance most successful applicants demonstrate. This combination made your profile riskier in the model's eyes, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority of students. While these predictions are data-driven, they're not destiny—many overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your recent grades if possible, and seeking mentorship or prep courses tailored for Black students navigating law school pathways. Building a stronger academic narrative could position you well for reapplication or alternative programs. Your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a middle-income background gives you a solid foundation to address these gaps.
